coredata.dc:title: Cellulose circular economy: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive vaccine indicators
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Shih-Chen
Chen, Xin-An
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose is a highly content natural polymer that can be extracted from plants. Rice straw is a valuable resource because it is rich in cellulose. This study aims to develop a technology to extract and utilize cellulose from rice straw, which would otherwise be discarded. Cellulose derived from rice straw is used to synthesize low-biotoxicity, high-performance graphene quantum dots. Cellulose is hydrolyzed into glucose and processed into graphene quantum dots using the hydrothermal method, which is a green synthetic route. The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide assay shows that the natural biomass (cellulose)-derived graphene quantum dots have low biological toxicity and cell viability that exceeds 100% (48 h, 400 micrograms per milliliter). Their luminescence properties, chemical stability, and biological toxicity are significantly improved by subsequent reduction and amination. Subsequently, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots are used as a display agent for vaccine detection, which quantified secondary antibody concentration with a detection limit of 2 pg/mL, excellent linearity (r 2 = 0.9982), and a low coefficient of variation of < 0.04%, outperforming the existing 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine. This study demonstrates that graphene quantum dots synthesized from natural polymers are suitable for low-toxicity applications, such as vaccine indicators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Circular economy
Green synthesis
Graphene quantum dot
Vaccine indicator

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective hydrolysis of cellulose into glucose
Selective hydrolysis of cellulose into glucose

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Science Technology Council, Taiwan (grant number MOST 110-2221-E-006-150, 111-2221-E-006-145, 111-2221-E-006-147-MY2, 111-2221-E-006-133, and 112-2221-E-006-173). The authors gratefully acknowledge the Core Facility Center, National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan. This research was supported in part by Higher Education Sprout Project, Ministry of Education to the Headquarters of University Advancement at National Cheng Kung University (NCKU).

This study was supported by the National Science Technology Council, Taiwan (grant number MOST 110-2221-E-006-150, 111-2221-E-006-145, 111-2221-E-006-147-MY2, 111-2221-E-006-133, and 112-2221-E-006-173). The authors gratefully acknowledge the Core Facility Center, National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selective hydrolysis of cellulose into glucose over AC-SO3H. (a) XRD patterns of cellulose after ball milling. (b) The DNS reagent is used to measure the concentration of glucose in each group by absorbance at 570 nm. (The solution used consists of a glucose powder with an aqueous solution of a specific concentration. The sample is the aqueous glucose solution obtained from cellulose hydrolysis). (c) FTIR detection of glucose obtained by cellulose hydrolysis by AC-SO3H. (Glucose: obtained by cellulose hydrolysis, Sample: commercially available glucose powder).
Examination of the properties of GQDs. (a) FTIR analysis of GQDs. (b) Raman spectrum of GQDs, and (c) HR-TEM image of GQDs (inset: magnified HR-TEM image of GQDs).

Chemical reduction of GQDs. (a) Photoexcited fluorescence spectra of GQDs reduced by different ascorbic acid amounts. (b) Photoexcited fluorescence spectra of GQDs with different hydrothermal reduction durations. (c–e) XPS analysis of GQDs: (c) as-grown GQDs, (d) GQDs reduced by ascorbic acid, and (e) GQDs reduced by ascorbic acid and a hydrothermal process.
PLE graphs of af-GQDs amino-functionalized at different temperatures: (a) 70, (b) 90, (c) 120, and (d) 150 °C.
Schematic of bonding changes at different stages of functionalization.
MTT assay of GQDs and af-GQDs at different concentrations (800, 400, 200, 100, and 50 micrograms per milliliter).
PL stability of GQDs and af-GQDs. Photodegradation of (a) GQDs and (b) af-GQDs after UV irradiation and the (c) PL of GQDs (bottom) and af-GQDs (top) at different pH levels.
The ability of (a) af-GQD and (b) TMB to quantify secondary antibodies.
Coefficient of variation (CV) at different secondary antibody concentrations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: af-QGD CV: amino-functionalized graphene quantum dot coefficient of variation

head.ce:title: Cellulose circular economy: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive vaccine indicators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No.1, University Road, Tainan 70101, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose is a highly content natural polymer that can be extracted from plants. Rice straw is a valuable resource because it is rich in cellulose. This study aims to develop a technology to extract and utilize cellulose from rice straw, which would otherwise be discarded. Cellulose derived from rice straw is used to synthesize low-biotoxicity, high-performance graphene quantum dots. Cellulose is hydrolyzed into glucose and processed into graphene quantum dots using the hydrothermal method, which is a green synthetic route. The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide assay shows that the natural biomass (cellulose)-derived graphene quantum dots have low biological toxicity and cell viability that exceeds 100% (48 h, 400 micrograms per milliliter). Their luminescence properties, chemical stability, and biological toxicity are significantly improved by subsequent reduction and amination. Subsequently, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots are used as a display agent for vaccine detection, which quantified secondary antibody concentration with a detection limit of 2 pg/mL, excellent linearity (r 2 = 0.9982), and a low coefficient of variation of < 0.04%, outperforming the existing 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine. This study demonstrates that graphene quantum dots synthesized from natural polymers are suitable for low-toxicity applications, such as vaccine indicators.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an emerging graphene nanomaterial with many excellent and unique properties, the applications of which have recently attracted the attention of many researchers in academia. In addition to edge and quantum confinement effects, GQDs have many unique physical and chemical properties, such as stable photoluminescence (PL), surface grafting, biocompatibility, inertness, solubility, nontoxicity, and tunable bandgaps (Tian et al., 2018). By exploiting these properties, GQDs can be used in various applications, such as bioimaging (Shen et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2011), sensing (Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2014; Raeyani et al., 2018), drug delivery (Chen et al., 2015; Qiu et al., 2015), photovoltaics (Tang et al., 2017; Tsai et al., 2016), photocatalysis (Arvand and Hemmati, 2017; Tang et al., 2018), photodynamic therapy (Hola et al., 2014), antimicrobial activity (Sun et al., 2014), and electroluminescence. Despite the many excellent properties of GQDs, several problems remain unsolved, including a low synthetic yield, low quantum yield, inability to precisely control the size, unclear PL mechanism, and narrow luminescence spectrum (Zhu et al., 2015). Among these aspects, their quantum yield has the greatest impact on applications. GQDs have a low PL intensity, which limits their applicability. This problem is typically solved by functionalizing GQDs, which can increase the electron density on their surface or edges, thereby significantly improving their PL intensity.

Cellulose is limited in its application owing to its compact structure and incommensurable decomposability (Rahmadiawan et al., 2023a; Rahmadiawan et al., 2023b; Shuai and Pan, 2012). Strong acids are often used to improve the efficiency during the hydrolysis of cellulose or the synthesis of nanoparticles. However, when such a process is applied to mass production, not only does using a large amount of strong acid significantly harm the environment, but the separation of the product and acid solution is also a major problem (Vilcocq et al., 2014). In recent years, green synthesis technologies have been actively developed to minimize environmental damage and optimize the reaction efficiency by using safer solvents and degradable products. Environmentally friendly technologies, such as solid acid catalyst hydrolysis, have been developed for the hydrolysis of cellulose. Solid catalysts generally have a high specific surface area that can improve the efficiency of cellulose hydrolysis. The hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds is conducted by providing hydrogen ions through the acid center point, and the product and acid can be separated easily and quickly after hydrolysis (Hu et al., 2015; Shuai and Pan, 2012). In this way, the disadvantage of using a large amount of sulfuric acid for hydrolysis, which has damaged the environment in the past, is improved.

Vaccines are injected into the human body through antigens to induce a specific immune response that can eliminate pathogens. This causes an immune memory in the human body, which allows the immune system to identify bacteria or viruses. The development of vaccines often requires the use of an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) to quantitatively analyze the number of antibodies on the antigen and identify its specificity (Clark et al., 1986). In the ELISA test, the display agent is connected to the secondary antibody to detect if the antibody is connected to the antigen. As such, the display agent must exhibit high sensitivity and low specificity. At present, the most commonly used display agent is 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), which has the disadvantages of acute toxicity and possibly causing genetic defects. GQDs have the advantages of many surface active sites, chemical stability, and high solubility, allowing them to react more stably and effectively without being affected by the background solution during quantitative analysis (Xiang et al., 2018). In addition, their high specific surface area, abundant functional groups, and easy functionalization ensure the excellent performance of GQDs as agents and signal amplification carriers in the development of immunosensors (Campuzano et al., 2019). Moreover, GQDs have extremely low biological toxicity and are emerging materials that have attracted much attention in biomedicine.

In this study, it was hypothesized that cellulose derived GQDs are highly luminescent, chemically stable, and biologically non-toxic. Furthermore, amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) are conjectured to be suitable for application in humans, such as vaccine detection, owing to their low toxicity and carcinogenicity.

This study extracts cellulose from rice straw via a green synthesis method (Shi and Liu, 2021). First, glucose is prepared by hydrolyzing cellulose. Subsequently, GQDs are synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Finally, the GQD is functionalized for toxicity tests and antibody detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective hydrolysis of cellulose into glucose
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.25 g of 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid and 75 g of Rochelle salt are dissolved in an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (50 mL, 2 M) and stirred for 30 min. The mixed solution is diluted to 250 mL with deionized water to obtain the dinitrosalicylic acid (DNS) reagent. Furthermore, 1 g of activated carbon powder is added to 20 mL of sulfuric acid with a concentration of 18 mol L−1. This solution is transferred to an autoclave and heated to 150 °C to decompose the sulfuric acid molecules into sulfo radicals that can react with activated carbon, which is a material with a high specific surface area and high hydrothermal stability, to obtain sulfonated activated carbon (AC-SO3H) after 16 h. A hydrothermal synthesis (200 °C) is subsequently conducted and the product is washed with distilled water until no sulfate ions remain. The final product is a solid acid catalyst capable of selectively hydrolyzing cellulose (Hu et al., 2015).

The crystal structure of cellulose is excessively tight, making it nonconductive to subsequent decomposition into small glucose molecules. As such, mechanical force is used to destroy the crystal structure. The product is subjected to zirconia ball milling for 48 h, such that the crystalline structure of the cellulose becomes loose and more –OH groups are exposed to the exterior of the structure. When the solid acid is subsequently hydrolyzed, the carboxyl groups (–COOH) on the solid acid catalyst can increase the efficiency of acid hydrolysis by adsorbing the –OH groups of cellulose. Next, 20 g of ball-milled cellulose is added into an autoclave with 50 mg of sulfonated activated carbon catalyst and 50 mL of deionized water, after which the mixture is heated to 200 °C and allowed to react at a constant temperature for 24 h. During this period, the sulfonated activated carbon catalyst releases a weak acid and selectively hydrolyzes cellulose into glucose molecules. Subsequently, the aqueous glucose solution and sulfonated activated carbon catalyst are separated by filtration through a 0.22 micrometer filter paper (Hu et al., 2015).

Further, 0.7 mL of a glucose aqueous solution with a known concentration is added to 7 mL of the DNS reagent and heated in a 100 °C water bath for 10 min. The mixture is then allowed to rest until it cools to 25 °C. A microplate reader is then used to measure the absorbance at a wavelength of 570 nm to calibrate the glucose concentration and absorbance. Subsequently, the glucose concentration can be deduced from the calibration curve by performing the same steps with the glucose aqueous solution obtained by hydrolysis of cellulose.

A glucose powder (70 mg) is mixed with deionized water (70 mL) and stirred. Then, the mixed solution is poured into a Teflon beaker and placed in an autoclave for hydrothermal synthesis. During the synthesis, constant stirring and introducing inert gas help prevent the inhomogeneous heating of the solution and reaction with oxygen. After the hydrothermal synthesis, impurities and by-products are removed by filtration (0.22 micrometer) and centrifugation (15,000 rpm, 30 min). The molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of the dialysis bag is 3500. The unreacted chemical substances are dialyzed out, such that the GQDs are retained in the dialysis membrane (Bayat and Saievar-Iranizad, 2017).

In the first step of chemical reduction, an aqueous solution of GQDs (35 mL) is mixed with ascorbic acid (35 mL, 20 wt%), stirred at room temperature for 10 min, and transferred to an oil bath (95 °C, 30 min). The final products are dialyzed (3500 MWCO) for 48 h, during which the water is changed every 4 h.

In the second step of chemical reduction, the dialyzed products are poured into a Teflon beaker and transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal reduction. The principle is that in the critical environment of a high temperature and pressure, water dissociates to produce hydrogen anions (H−), which provides water with a reducing capability to remove the oxygen functional groups on the surface of GQDs, so as to obtain reduced graphene quantum dots (r-GQDs) (Luo et al., 2014).

Reduced GQDs (25 mL), aqueous ammonia (15 mL, 28 wt%), and DI water (25 mL) are mixed and stirred at room temperature for 20 min; the mixture is then poured into a Teflon beaker and transferred to an autoclave for a hydrothermal reaction (5 h) at 70, 90, 120, or 150 °C. Thereafter, the solution is removed from the autoclave, placed on a heating stirrer, and all unreacted aqueous ammonia is volatilized by heating to obtain af-GQDs (Tetsuka et al., 2012).

The medium comprises a mixture of four substances. Dulbecco’s modified minimal essential medium (DMEM) is first mixed with f-12 medium at a ratio of 1:1 to form a DMEM/f-12 medium, which is then mixed with fetal bovine serum and antibiotics (p/s) at a ratio of 90:10:1.

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) reagent comprises MTT powder and DMEM mixed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at a concentration of 300 micrograms per milliliter. Frozen vero cells are thawed in a constant-temperature water tank (37 °C, 30 min), and microscopy is performed to determine if the cells are in adequate condition. Then, the old medium is removed and the cells are washed twice with PBS. After washing off the excess medium, trypsin is added to detach the cells from the bottom of the Petri dish. The Petri dish is placed into an incubator (37 °C, 5 min), after which DMEM (3 mL) is added to stop the reaction between the trypsin and cells. The cell solution is finally transferred from the Petri dish to a centrifuge tube for centrifugation (1000 rpm, 3 min). The supernatant is removed so that only the cells remain, which are redissolved in DMEM. The cells are then counted with a cell counter. If the target number is not reached, the cells are placed into a new Petri dish to complete the first subculture. This process is repeated until the target number of cells is reached, upon which the cells can be reconstituted into a 96-well plate (106 cell/100 micro liter).

GQDs and af-GQDs at five different concentrations (800, 400, 200, 100, and 50 micro gramme/ micro liter) are co-cultured with the cells, and each test with the same parameters is repeated three times.

After the sample is co-cultured with the cells, the medium is removed and a new medium (100 micro liter) is added. After adding the MTT reagent (10 micro liter), the 96-well plate is placed into an incubator at 25 °C for 3 h. Then, the plate is removed from the incubator and the medium and reagent are removed. At this time, water-insoluble purple crystals formed by the reaction between the MTT reagent and cells remain at the bottom. Subsequently, 15 micro liter of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is added to dissolve these crystals and the 96-well plate is again transferred to the incubator (25 °C, 10 min). Because the amount of crystalline material formed is proportional to the number of living cells, the amount can be determined by measuring the absorbance (540 nm) with a microplate reader and further quantifying the activity of mitochondria in cells.

The structure of the cellulose is resolved using grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS Gmbh, D8 Discover Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). The attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the materials are recorded by an FTIR Spectrometer (Nicolet iS5, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). All ATR-FTIR samples are crushed into small particles, combined with KBr, and pressed into small disks. The morphology, size, and structure of the GQDs are characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, voltage of 100 KV). In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses are conducted to determine the structure of the synthesized GQDs using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe analyzer with monochromatic Cu–126 Kα radiation.

In this application, the synthesized af-GQDs are used for replacing TMB, which is widely used to detect the specificity of antibodies. The standard ELISA procedure is as follows: adhere the antigen onto the bottom of a 96-well plate with a coating buffer and add the antibody to it for bonding. The secondary antibody is subsequently added to bond with the antibody to detect the grafting status of the antibody. Finally, TMB is bonded to the secondary antibody, which becomes colored. The specificity of antibody and antigen is obtained by detecting the concentration of TMB by absorbance.

Because the secondary antibody must bond to the primary antibody, this study only aims to determine if af-GQDs can bond to the secondary antibody. In this experiment, the secondary antibodies of different concentrations are adhered to the bottom of a 96-well plate with a coating buffer and af-GQDs are added to bond with the secondary antibodies. The 96-well plate is rinsed with PBS to remove the excess af-GQDs. The secondary antibodies and af-GQDs are removed and the absorbance is measured with a microplate reader to determine if the concentration of the secondary antibodies follows a linear trend. If this trend is linear, af-GQDs are determined to have bonded well with the secondary antibodies, rendering it possible to quantitatively analyze the concentration of the secondary antibodies. Finally, the performance of the af-GQDs and TMB is evaluated to quantify the secondary antibodies and compare them with respect to the concentration detection limit and coefficient of variation as indicators.

The test for PL stability can be divided into two parts. In the first part, GQDs are degraded by UV-light irradiation. In this part, an LED is used as the source of UV light. The GQDs before and after functionalization are continuously and directly irradiated for 15, 30, 45, and 60 min at room temperature. The difference in the degree of light decay for different durations of UV irradiation and GQD functionalization states are observed. The second part is the fluorescence quenching of the GQDs at different pH levels. The pH level affects the PL behavior of the GQDs. For GQDs in acid or alkali environments, the transition electrons of each bond cause charge transfer through H+ and OH−, resulting in a sharp decrease in the PL intensity. This phenomenon is referred to as fluorescence quenching. As such, this part of the test adjusts the pH level (from pH 1 to pH 14) and the changes in the PL intensity before and after functionalization at different pH levels are observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective hydrolysis of cellulose into glucose
ce:section.ce:para: As cellulose is a chain structure with a high crystalline strength, a part of the crystalline segment of cellulose must be loosened by mechanical force to allow the glycosidic bond of the cellulose to be effectively hydrolyzed by acid. As shown in Fig. 1a, the ball-milled cellulose exhibits a significantly reduced crystalline peak intensity at 22.5°, indicating that the crystallinity of the cellulose has decreased. As such, the next step of the selective hydrolysis of a solid acid can be performed.

Cellulose and sulfonated activated carbon are mixed in deionized water and placed in an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. During this process, the sulfo groups selectively break the β1–4 glycosidic bond to hydrolyze it into glucose molecules. In the experiment, the concentration of each glucose aqueous solution is determined using the DNS reagent, including that of the known concentration (Experiment) and that hydrolyzed from cellulose (Sample), as shown in Fig. 1b. The absorbance is interpolated based on the calibration curve and the concentration of the glucose is calculated to be 0.4409 mg/mL. ATR-FTIR is used for the structural characterization, as shown in Fig. 1c. The –OH peak at 3300 cm−1, C–H peak at 2947 cm−1, and C–O peaks at 1149, 1108, and 1085 cm−1 all correspond to the stretching vibrations of these bonds. These are standard characteristic peaks of glucose, thereby validating that the reduced sugar is glucose.

In the hydrothermal environment, –OH and –H on the glucose molecules react with –H and –OH of other molecules under the dehydration reaction, as shown in Fig. 2a. At the same time, two different glucose molecules bond with the carbon–carbon bond and self-assemble into graphene fragments, i.e., GQDs.

The amount of structural defects is defined by the ID/IG ratio of the Raman spectrum, where ID represents the intensity of the peak corresponding to structural defects of sp3 hybridization, and IG represents that of the graphene structure of sp2 hybridization. As such, the larger the ratio, the more structural defects there are, which are presumably derived from oxygen vacancies formed by excessive reaction with oxygen. In this study, the synthesis yield optimization and defect minimization of GQDs synthesized by glucose hydrothermal synthesis are conducted by adjusting the parameters of hydrothermal synthesis (environment, temperature, and time). The synthetic yield of the optimal group is 24.67%, with a structural defect ratio (ID/IG) of 0.778, as shown in Fig. 2b. HR-TEM shows that the GQDs have a crystalline structure, as shown in Fig. 2c. If the synthesized material is graphene oxide, no crystalline structure appears. Analysis using the ImageJ software package indicates that the structure constant is 0.24 nm, the edge is shaped like a zigzag, and the average particle sizes are 2–4 nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2c. The structural constant confirms that the material is a GQD. If the material was a carbon nanodot, its structure constant would be 0.36 nm, which corresponds to (002) graphite crystals.

When GQDs are synthesized, many oxygen functional groups, such as C–OH, C–O–C, and CO, are bound to their edges and planes. These bonds are defect states within the graphene structure, which fall in the forbidden band, belonging to a relatively low-energy state; consequently, the PL intensity is also low. Therefore, the removal of these low-energy defect states through reduction increases the PL intensity. The higher the degree of reduction, the higher the PL intensity. In the experiment, GQDs are first reduced with ascorbic acid. With the same reduction time, the optimal amount of ascorbic acid that maximizes the PL intensity is determined by adding different amounts of ascorbic acid, as shown in Fig. 3a, and the GQDs reduced by ascorbic acid are designated as r-GQDs-A. The r-GQDs-A are transferred to an autoclave in the second step of reduction, which are reduced by the hydrogen anions dissociated from water molecules. The hydrothermal reduction time is used as the parameter to maximize the PL intensity, yielding the results shown in Fig. 3b. The GQDs after hydrothermal reduction are designated as r-GQDs-H. The XPS elemental analysis shown in Fig. 3c–e implies that ascorbic acid and hydrothermal reductions can effectively remove oxygen functional groups, as the C/O ratio increases from the original 0.614–1.473 and 2.569, respectively, after the two reductions. Furthermore, the ratio of carboxyl groups (–COOH) on the surface of the GQDs increases during the reduction.

The changes in the surface functional groups of the GQDs before and after amination are compared by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4. The af-GQDs show two additional peaks on the FTIR spectrum at 3150 and 1315 cm−1. The peak at 3150 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of N–H, while the peak at 1315 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of C–N, which proves that the amino group is bound to the GQDs.

The PL excitation (PLE) graphs are shown in Fig. 5 for amino functionalization at different temperatures. The four graphs reveal that the optimal excitation wavelength for the temperature of 70, 90, and 120 °C is 320 nm and that of 150 °C is 300 nm. The corresponding optimal emission wavelengths are 424, 412, 412, and 364 nm, indicating the occurrence of a blue shift. PLE analysis shows that the blue shift of the emission wavelength is due to the increase in the ratio of amino groups bonded to carboxyl groups, causing more electrons to transition to higher energy states, thus increasing the energy gap. The functional groups and bonding changes on GQDs at different stages are shown in Fig. 6.

Graphene quantum dots without chemical modification were measured for their quantum yield using an integrating sphere. Compared to graphene quantum dots prepared by the documented process, there was an increase of 0.02% in quantum yield (Sun et al., 2015). Amino-functionalized GQD significantly improves the luminescence performance. The ratio of the number of absorbed photons and the number of emitted photons is determined by integrating spheres, as shown in Figure below. It can be seen that compared with the reduced graphene quantum dots, its quantum yield is increased by 1.03%, which also represents that under the absorption of the same number of photons, the number of photons emitted becomes 236% of the original.

Vero cells are selected for the MTT colorimetry of GQDs and af-GQDs, and the effects of different concentrations before and after amino functionalization on cell viability are detected. GQDs have an inherently low biological toxicity. To demonstrate that the GQDs synthesized from cellulose (the natural biomass adopted in this study) have a lower biotoxicity, the cell detection results are compared with those from other studies using the same detection method. The experimental parameters are the different concentrations of GQDs (800, 400, 200, 100, or 50 micrograms per milliliter) and co-culture duration of GQDs and cells (1 or 2 d), as shown in Fig. 7. The results of the MTT assay show that the GQDs produced in this study have an extremely low biological toxicity. In the reference literature, cells co-cultured with 50 micrograms per milliliter of GQDs for 1 or 2 d have a cell viability of 28% or 33%. In contrast, when co-cultured with the GQDs in this study for 1 d, a distinct biological toxicity is only detected when the concentration reaches 400 micrograms per milliliter, and the cell viability is 22% when the concentration is as high as 800 micrograms per milliliter. When the co-culture duration is 2 d, no distinct biological toxicity is detected when the concentration is 400 micrograms per milliliter. The MTT assay results show that the toxicity of the GQDs to cells can be reduced by the amino functionalization. When co-cultured for 1 d, af-GQDs still exhibit a distinct cytotoxicity at a concentration of 400 micrograms per milliliter, yet the cell viability with 800 micrograms per milliliter GQDs is higher than that with 50 micrograms per milliliter GQDs in the reference literature. When co-cultured for 2 d, the cell viability is enhanced from 4% to 52% by af-GQDs at a concentration of 800 micrograms per milliliter, significantly reducing the biological toxicity. This is because cells wither because of the high concentration of GQDs. In contrast, the af-GQDs can induce cell autophagy, such that cells that would otherwise wither can be regenerated, ultimately improving cell viability. Thus, this phenomenon is most distinct at the highest concentration of 800 micrograms per milliliter.

Fluorescent probes used in biological imaging must have a low biological toxicity, high PL stability, and resistance to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the PL stability is tested in this part of the experiment. The GQDs are continuously irradiated with UV light and the ability to resist light decay before and after amino functionalization is observed. Fig. 8 shows that the PL intensity of GQDs decreases as the UV-light irradiation duration increases. Compared with the original GQDs, the PL intensity of the af-GQDs decreases by only 9.5% after 60 min of UV irradiation (Fig. 8a), while that for GQDs decreases by 21.1% (Fig. 8b). Thus, the presence of amino groups improves the ability of GQDs to confine photons within the structure. This characteristic allows the unlimited application of GQDs. For example, poor PL stability causes the PL intensity to decline to an unobservable level after a few scans in fluorescence imaging. In contrast, the af-GQDs synthesized in this study can retain more than 90% of the PL intensity under continuous UV irradiation for 60 min

GQDs exhibit the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching in an acidic environment. This is because the surface functional groups are protonated in an acidic environment and the electrons are transferred accordingly, resulting in a significant decrease in the PL intensity. This phenomenon limits certain biomedical applications, such as drug delivery in tumor cells. If GQDs are used as drug carriers, the PL intensity will decrease owing to the reduced pH level near the tumor cells. Therefore, the PL performance of af-GQDs at different pH levels is tested in this part of the experiment and the degree of fluorescence quenching caused by different pH levels before and after amino functionalization is observed. First, the r-GQDs (Fig. 8c (bottom)) are observed with an initial pH level of 7. At acid–base neutrality, the PL intensity is relatively high. As the pH level decreases or increases, the surface of the GQDs is protonated or deprotonated, respectively, both of which cause a sharp decrease in the surface electron density, leading to a decrease in the final PL intensity. The changes in the PL intensity of af-GQDs at different pH levels are also shown (Fig. 8c (top)) with an initial pH level of 8. The same trend can be observed, as the PL intensity is relatively high at acid–base neutrality. Nevertheless, af-GQDs are different from r-GQDs in that a high PL intensity is maintained in the pH range from 1 to 14. This proves that amino functionalization allows GQDs to resist fluorescence quenching, such that GQDs can exert their effects in a wider range of pH levels.

A two-fold serial dilution is performed nine times on the secondary antibody at an initial concentration of 1000 pg/mL, such that the lowest concentration is 1.9531 pg/mL. In this detection range, the absorbance has a very good linear relationship with the concentration (r 2 = 0.99816) ( Fig. 9a), such that the minimum detection limit that can be measured at present is 1.9531 pg/mL. For TMB detected by a microplate reader, the degree of linearity between the TMB detection results and concentration of the secondary antibody is slightly higher than that of the af-GQDs detection results (r 2 = 0.99873) (Fig. 9b), for which the minimum detection limit is also 1.9531 pg/mL. This proves that the GQDs with amino groups have the same bonding ability to the secondary antibody as TMB, as the degrees of linearity are similar. In addition to analyzing the limit of the concentration of secondary antibodies detected by the display reagents, the analysis of the coefficient of variation (CV) is also conducted. The objective herein is the detection of the absorbance stability value of each reagent after labeling the secondary antibody. If the CV is large, the changes in the detection results of each group are large with the same parameters. In other words, the smaller the CV is, the higher the detection accuracy of the display agent will be. In vaccine testing, the CV of each group of parameters should generally be less than 15%. Data analysis results for the secondary antibodies detected by af-GQDs and TMB are shown in Table 1. The CV of TMB is very low for the detection at a low concentration. When the concentration is 31.25 pg/mL, the CV is smaller than 0.01%. As the concentration of secondary antibodies increases, the value of the CV also increases accordingly. When the concentration reaches 1000 pg/mL, the CV exceeds 15%, leading to unqualified detection results. However, when af-GQDs are the display agent, the CV is less than 0.02% at most concentrations. The CV value is less than 0.04% for all concentrations, which indicates that af-GQDs are more stable in quantifying the concentration of secondary antibodies than TMB.

GQDs synthesized from cellulose have a low biotoxicity and functionalizing their amino groups can further decrease their biotoxicity. The presence of amino groups on the surface and edges of GQDs induced autophagy in cells, increasing cell activity when co-cultured with cells. Compared with that of cells cultured with 800 micrograms per milliliter unmodified GQDs for 48 h, the cell activity of those cultured with af-GQDs was 48% higher. The fluorescence characteristics of af-GQDs were used to detect the concentration of secondary antibodies. Compared with TMB, af-GQDs were better suited to quantifying the concentration of secondary antibodies in the concentration range of 2–1000 pg/mL. Using cellulose as a raw material would significantly decrease costs, simplify the synthetic route, and accelerate the commercialization of GQDs. However, the green synthesis process of af-GQDs currently has a low yield and requires further optimization. Nonetheless, af-GQDs are not carcinogenic or biotoxic and may be a suitable alternative for TMB.
Shih-Chen Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xin-An Chen: Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Science Technology Council, Taiwan (grant number MOST 110-2221-E-006-150, 111-2221-E-006-145, 111-2221-E-006-147-MY2, 111-2221-E-006-133, and 112-2221-E-006-173). The authors gratefully acknowledge the Core Facility Center, National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan. This research was supported in part by Higher Education Sprout Project, Ministry of Education to the Headquarters of University Advancement at National Cheng Kung University (NCKU).
